quotes.verbatim_quote:
A diatonic enclosure is when you play one note above (the 9), then one note below (the 7), and
finally your target note (the 1).
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the core melodic device—diatonic enclosure—that Pattern 1 and much of the method's bebop vocabulary is built around.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Put on a backing track and start improvising, starting each phrase with the pattern you want to
practice.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the method's prescribed drill for internalizing any pattern, making it the universal technique-acquisition rule for the entire book.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
A good exercise to get different positions of a pattern under your fingers is by playing it through the
circle of fifths.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This establishes the circle-of-fifths run-through as the standard method for learning all fretboard positions of any pattern.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Note that the pattern uses a natural 7 instead of the b7 that normally occurs in minor scales. The
natural 7 can be seen as either a chromatic note or the 7 of the melodic minor scale.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This rule explains a defining bebop alteration—using the natural 7 over minor chords—and gives the theoretical justification for it.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
There are two typical bebop characteristics in this lick:

1. The triplet going from b9 to #9 to b9 with a hammer-on and pull-off.
2. The b6 interval starting from the 3rd of the dominant chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This enumerates the two defining bebop devices embedded in Pattern 3, teaching both the rhythmic technique (hammer-on/pull-off triplet) and the intervallic move (b6 from the 3rd).

quotes.verbatim_quote:
An augmented 7th arpeggio or triad is a convenient way to bring out an altered sound on dominant
chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is a concise rule linking the augmented arpeggio to altered dominant harmony, which is foundational to jazz improvisation vocabulary.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Playing a half-diminished arpeggio from the 3rd of a dominant chord brings out the 9 sound.
For example: Bm7b5 over G7.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the Bm7b5-over-G7 substitution rule with its harmonic rationale, a reusable device that recurs in Patterns 5, 19, and 55.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
On a minor ii V I, you can play the tonic harmonic minor scale over the V7alt chord.
For example: over Bm7b5-E7alt-Am7 (ii VI in A minor), you play A harmonic minor over the

E7alt chord.

When doing so, you create a 7b9,b13 sound over that chord.

Another name for the harmonic minor scale played over a dominant chord is the Phrygian dominant
scale.

| A harmonic minor = E Phrygian dominant
quotes.why_load_bearing: This passage defines the harmonic minor / Phrygian dominant equivalence and the specific alteration tones it produces, giving players a concrete scale-choice rule for minor ii-V-I contexts.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Pattern 18 uses a simple, but effective technique that I learned from transcribing Pat Metheny solos:

doubling notes.

It can be applied to every scale, arpeggio, or pattern you can think of.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This introduces the doubling-notes technique as a universally applicable melodic tool, not just a one-off pattern, expanding its relevance across the entire method.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You can play a maj7 chord over a half-diminished chord, starting on the b5 of that half-
diminished chord. For example: Fmaj7 over Bm7b5
quotes.why_load_bearing: This states the generalized substitution rule for coloring half-diminished chords with a major-7 arpeggio, producing the beautiful #11 tension.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The triad brings out a sus4 sound (C) with the altered tensions b9 (Ab) and b13 (Eb).
quotes.why_load_bearing: This explains exactly which chord tones and tensions the Ab minor triad generates over G7, giving players a harmonic rationale for the substitution.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Pattern 26 is a typical Pat Metheny phrase, where the 16th notes are played with a hammer on and
two pull offs.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This specifies the precise right-hand/left-hand technique required to execute Pattern 26's characteristic 16th-note figure.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Playing Dm over G7 is a technique Wes Montgomery would often use in his solos.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This names a specific stylistic substitution—Dm triad over G7—attributing it to Wes Montgomery and validating its use in Pattern 25's dominant context.
